theory.canonical_statement: Every navigation system requires two things: a coordinate frame and a reference point. GPS uses latitude, longitude, and altitude — three numbers that locate any point on Earth relative to a fixed grid. Star charts use right ascension and declination — two numbers that locate any point in the sky relative to the celestial equator. The 129,600 Navigation System begins from a different premise. The reference point is not a fixed external grid. The reference point is you. The coordinate frame expands outward from the observer, scaling from the interior of a single cell to the radius of the observable universe. The 129,600 points on the sphere are not geographic locations — they are directions of meaning, each encoding one of 129,600 distinct modes of engagement between a self and a world.
theory.technical_interpretation: interpretable coordinates. **They are two atlases on the same sphere.** The physics runs on the Fibonacci lattice; the human interface uses the rectangular lattice. ### Scalability: From Cell to Galaxy The defining feature of the 129,600 Navigation System is not its resolution — it is its scalability. From any observer point $\mathcal{O}$, the sphere of directions radiates outward. The 129,600 nodes are directional addresses — they specify not a distance but a bearing. Adding a scale parameter $r$ converts each directional address into a spatial point at distance $r$ from $\mathcal{O}$: where $\hat{n}_{ij}$ is the unit vector corresponding to node $(i, j)$ in the $360 \times 360$ grid. **The same 129,600 directional addresses apply at every scale.** The navigation system does not change when $r$ changes — only the physical scale of the space being navigated does. This means the system ca In technical terms, the entry packages the selected formula as the operative carrier, the nearby equations as the progression layer, and the symbol key as the compact interpretation of variables used by the source.

dictionary_sections.definition: The 129,600 Navigation System is a source-backed Opera Omnia registry packet centered on the canonical relation P_{ij}(r) = \mathcal{O} + r\,\hat{n}_{ij}. It identifies the mathematical spine of the cited chapter and gives the entry a concrete equation, symbol key, and derivation chain for the four-page production layout.
dictionary_sections.contextual_meaning: Every navigation system requires two things: a coordinate frame and a reference point. GPS uses latitude, longitude, and altitude — three numbers that locate any point on Earth relative to a fixed grid. Star charts use right ascension and declination — two numbers that locate any point in the sky relative to the celestial equator. The 129,600 Navigation System begins from a different premise. The reference point is not a fixed external grid. The reference point is you. The 129,600 points on the sphere are not geographic locations — they are directions of meaning, each encoding one of 129,600 distinct modes of engagement between a self and a world. The entry should be read as part of the larger Opera Omnia progression: a local equation is selected, its surrounding prose supplies the physical or mathematical role, and the production packet keeps that source connection visible.
dictionary_sections.derivable_consequences: The equation progression records P_{ij}(r) = \mathcal{O} + r\,\hat{n}_{ij}; 360 \times 360 = 129{,}600; C(\psi_\text{departure}, \psi_\text{birth}) = |\langle\psi_\text{departure}|\psi_\text{birth}\rangle|/(\|\psi_\text{departure}\|\cdot\|\psi_\text{birth}\|); \mathcal{O}. These relations give the support page enough structure to show how the canonical formula arises from nearby source material rather than from a disconnected title alone.
